source: Comissão Nacional da Verdade (CNV), Final Report 2014
note: CNV confirmed 434 deaths and forced disappearances. Actual numbers likely higher; many cases remain unresolved. No perpetrators have been prosecuted due to the 1979 Amnesty Law, upheld by STF in 2010 and condemned by the Inter-American Court in the Gomes Lund case.

Total Brazilian military and civilian deaths disputed among historians. Paraguay lost 60–70% of its male population (est. 300,000+). Brazil suffered massive casualties from combat and especially cholera and yellow fever. The war accelerated the Empire's decline and eventually led to the 1889 republic.

source: Euclides da Cunha, 'Os Sertões' (1902); Calasans, José. 'Canudos na Literatura de Cordel'
note: After four military expeditions, the federal army destroyed Canudos and killed virtually all inhabitants. Antônio Conselheiro's millenarian community was annihilated. Euclydes da Cunha's eyewitness account 'Os Sertões' is the foundational literary and historical record. The lake formed by the Cocorobó dam now submerges the original site.

source: IHGB; Cleary, David. 'Toward an Environmental History of the Amazon' (2001)
note: The Cabanagem was one of the deadliest popular revolts in Latin American history. Led by mixed-race caboclos, indigenous people, and enslaved Africans against Portuguese colonial élites in what is now Pará and Amazonas. The revolt killed an estimated 30–40% of the provincial population of Grão-Pará.

injured: ~23 million confirmed cases; hundreds of thousands hospitalized
source: Ministério da Saúde / DATASUS; Fiocruz epidemiological reports (2022)
note: Brazil had the second-highest COVID-19 death toll globally (after the USA). The Bolsonaro government's rejection of vaccines, promotion of unproven treatments (hydroxychloroquine, ivermectin), and opposition to lockdowns are linked by Brazil's Senate COVID CPI (2021) to preventable excess mortality. Independent epidemiological estimates range from 100,000 to 400,000 preventable deaths.

source: SEMAD Minas Gerais; Defesa Civil do Estado de MG (2019); Vale SA settlement agreement (2021)
note: Vale SA's Córrego do Feijão mine dam failed on January 25, 2019, releasing 12 million m³ of iron ore tailings. The deadliest mine dam disaster in Brazilian history. Vale reached a R$37.7 billion (≈$7 billion) settlement with MG state in 2021. Criminal charges against 16 Vale executives and 5 TÜV SÜD employees remain under prosecution.

injured: ~650 displaced; entire Doce River ecosystem destroyed
source: IBAMA; Samarco / ANA joint environmental assessment (2016); Renova Foundation
note: Samarco's Fundão tailings dam in Mariana, MG failed November 5, 2015, releasing 40–60 million m³ of iron ore waste into the Doce River — Brazil's worst environmental disaster in territorial scale. The mud plume reached the Atlantic Ocean 650 km downstream. Total remediation costs estimated at R$100 billion. Samarco is a joint venture of BHP Billiton and Arcelormittal.

source: CIMI (Conselho Indigenista Missionário), 'Violência Contra os Povos Indígenas no Brasil' annual reports (2019–2022)
note: CIMI reports document murders, invasions, and territorial violations against indigenous communities, with violence spiking under Bolsonaro's loosening of FUNAI protections and IBAMA enforcement. The Yanomami health crisis of 2023 — declared a humanitarian emergency by Lula on January 20, 2023 — revealed malnutrition and mercury poisoning from illegal gold mining (garimpo).

category: Rio de Janeiro Police Lethality (2020–2024 average)
injured: Tens of thousands; high injury rates in favela communities
source: Instituto de Segurança Pública (ISP-RJ); Amnesty International Brazil reports
note: Rio de Janeiro state police are among the world's most lethal per capita, killing approximately 1,700–2,000 people per year in official 'auto de resistência' (resistance/killed in action) shootings. The 2021 STF ruling in ADPF 635 (PSOL v. Rio de Janeiro) ordered restrictions on favela operations during sensitive periods. The 2021 Jacarezinho operation (28 killed in a single raid) was ruled partially unlawful by STF.
